intro: The clearest split between Clerk and Stytch is how they charge: Clerk uses a free tier plus paid plans starting around $25/mo (Pro, 10K MAU), while Stytch uses usage-based pricing at $0.10/user/mo. Clerk is Drop-in authentication platform with prebuilt React components for sign-in, MFA, organization management, and SSO; it fits best for apps, Startup. Stytch is Authentication API with passkeys, magic links, OTPs, OAuth, and enterprise SSO in a single developer platform; it fits best for Apps, B2B. Pick Clerk when the job maps to next.js / react apps needing drop-in auth ui; pick Stytch when it maps to apps needing sso/mfa. Clerk is not for enterprise requiring saml/scim on day one. Stytch is not for internal, static projects. Honest trade-offs: Clerk is weak for enterprise requiring saml/scim on day one — auth0/workos, and Stytch is weak for internal tools with basic auth — match to your real scenario. Both sit under the Identity / Auth / User Management umbrella, so your final call depends on pricing transparency, integration effort, and which listed not-for situations you can actually tolerate.
quick_take: Clerk is for apps, startup; Stytch is for apps, b2b; decide based on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Clerk if you want a free tier plus paid plans starting around $25/mo (Pro, 10K MAU) and your workload matches apps, startup.
verdict_b: Choose Stytch if you want usage-based pricing at $0.10/user/mo and your workload matches apps, b2b.

faq.a: A direct comparison depends on your usage profile and which pricing model fits better. Clerk has a free tier with paid plans starting at $25/mo (Pro, 10K MAU). Stytch uses usage-based pricing at $0.10/user/mo.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Clerk to Stytch?
faq.a: Migration is workable but not trivial, since both sit around medium lock-in. Plan for data export, re-integration of SDKs, and re-testing flows before cutover. Verify exportable formats and feature parity against your live use cases before committing.

Both score 5/5 on developer experience in our data. Evaluate against your own stack: read their docs, run a quick SDK spike, and check error handling. Self-assessing with a small prototype beats trusting any rating at face value.

faq.q: Is Stytch a good alternative to Clerk?
faq.a: Stytch sits in the same category as Clerk, so it can be a reasonable alternative for overlapping use cases. Stytch is best fit for apps, b2b. Note: Stytch is not for internal, static projects.
